options.explain: Post-processing a raw blob is brittle — deterministic code has to handle every format permutation. Better to let the model normalize at extraction time.

options.text: Request multiple extraction attempts per document and select the most common format.
options.explain: Ensemble voting is expensive and can still pick a wrong-format majority.

options.text: Define a strict output schema and include format normalization rules in your prompt.
options.explain: Correct. Strict schema + explicit normalization rules ('prices as decimal with two places', 'dietary as enumerated tags') lets the model both extract and normalize in one pass.

text:
Your system extracts event metadata (date, location, organizer, `attendee_count`) from news articles using a JSON schema with all nullable fields. During evaluation, you observe the model frequently generates plausible but incorrect values for fields not mentioned in the article—for example, outputting "500" for `attendee_count` when the source contains no attendance information.
What's the most effective way to reduce these false extractions?

options.text: Add a post-processing step using a second LLM call to verify each extracted value exists in the source document.
options.explain: Expensive second pass for a behavior you can correct at the source — tell the model to return null when the field isn't stated.

options.text: Add prompt instructions to return null for any field where information is not directly stated in the source.
options.explain: Correct. The fields are already nullable; the model just needs an explicit instruction to prefer null over a plausible guess. This is the standard fix for schema-aware hallucination.

options.text: Make all schema fields required (non-nullable) with strict validation rules to ensure the model only outputs verifiable data.
options.explain: Required fields force the model to invent values when information is missing — the opposite of what you want.

options.text: Upgrade to a more capable model tier with improved instruction-following to reduce hallucination tendencies.
options.explain: Adds cost and still doesn't tell the model your policy — returning null instead of inventing.

text:
After implementing tool use with strict schema definitions, JSON syntax errors are eliminated, but 5% of extractions still have valid JSON with empty arrays or null values for required fields like citations and methodology. Spot-checking reveals that source documents contain this information, but in varied formats—inline citations vs. bibliographies, methodology sections vs. details embedded in introductions.
What's the most effective way to address these failures?

options.text: Implement retry logic that re-sends requests when validation detects empty required fields.
options.explain: Retry on the same prompt won't suddenly teach the model to recognize methodology embedded in an intro. The extraction capability is the gap.

options.text: Build a regex-based post-processing layer that scans source documents for citation patterns and methodology keywords, populating empty fields when the model fails to extract.
options.explain: Regex over prose is brittle, misses the nuanced cases (intro-embedded methodology), and ships silently wrong data.

options.text: Modify your schema to make citations and methodology optional, and flag incomplete records for manual review rather than failing validation.
options.explain: Papers over the symptom. The fields really are required downstream — routing them to humans trades one problem for reviewer workload.

options.text: Add few-shot examples demonstrating extractions from documents with varied structures—showing how to identify citations in different formats and locate methodology details across section types.
options.explain: Correct. The failure mode is the model not recognizing varied formats. Concrete examples across the format distribution directly raise recall on the 5%.

text:
Your extraction pipeline processes invoices and extracts line items, subtotals, tax amounts, and grand totals. During evaluation, you discover that in 18% of extractions, the sum of extracted line item amounts doesn't match the extracted grand total—sometimes due to OCR errors in the source document, sometimes due to extraction mistakes by the model. Downstream accounting systems reject records with mismatched totals.
What's the most effective approach to improve extraction reliability?

options.text: Add a "`calculated_total`" field where the model sums extracted line items alongside a "`stated_total`" field. Flag records for human review when values differ.
options.explain: Correct. Capturing both values makes the discrepancy a first-class signal — you catch OCR errors and extraction mistakes uniformly, and you can route only the mismatched 18% to humans.

options.text: Extract line items and totals independently, then use a separate validation model to reconcile discrepancies by determining which extracted values are most likely correct.
options.explain: A reconciliation model can mask OCR errors by silently picking one number over another — the accounting system still gets wrong data without a flag.

options.text: Add few-shot examples demonstrating invoices where extracted line items sum correctly to the stated total, encouraging the model to produce mathematically consistent extractions.
options.explain: Can nudge the model toward consistency, but when the source itself is internally inconsistent (OCR errors), the model has to choose one 'wrong'.

options.text: Implement post-processing that automatically adjusts line item amounts proportionally when their sum doesn't match the stated total.
options.explain: Silent financial data rewrites are dangerous — you'd be fabricating line items for a downstream accounting system.

text:
Your pipeline uses a tool called `extract_metadata` with a JSON schema for paper details. You've also defined `lookup_citations` and `verify_doi` tools for enrichment. During testing, you notice that when users include requests like "extract the metadata and tell me how cited it is," Claude sometimes calls `lookup_citations` first, which fails because it needs the DOI that `extract_metadata` would provide.
What's the most effective way to ensure structured metadata extraction happens first?

options.text: Set `tool_choice` to "any" so Claude must use a tool, combined with system prompt instructions prioritizing `extract_metadata`.
options.explain: 'any' forces a tool call but doesn't force the right one. You'd still see `lookup_citations` picked first.

options.text: Set `tool_choice` to "auto" and reorder the tool definitions so `extract_metadata` appears first in the tools array, since Claude prioritizes earlier-listed tools.
options.explain: There's no documented ordering preference to rely on; this assumes behavior that isn't contractual.

options.text: Set `tool_choice` to {"type": "tool", "name": "`extract_metadata`"} and process the enrichment requests in subsequent turns after receiving the extracted metadata.
options.explain: Correct. `tool_choice`=specific-tool deterministically forces `extract_metadata` on the first turn. Then you hand control back to 'auto' to let the model use citations/DOI enrichment with the metadata in context.

options.text: Set `tool_choice` to {"type": "tool", "name": "`extract_metadata`"} for every API call in the pipeline, ensuring Claude always extracts metadata before any enrichment can occur.
options.explain: Pinning `extract_metadata` on every call prevents the enrichment tools from ever being used.

text:
Your extraction uses tool use with a JSON schema where `property_type` is defined as an enum: ['house', 'apartment', 'condo', 'townhouse']. After deployment, 8% of extractions fail schema validation. Investigation reveals listings mention many uncommon property types—"studio", "loft", "duplex", "mobile home", "tiny house", "converted warehouse"—and new types continue appearing regularly.
What's the most effective long-term solution?

options.text: Continuously expand the enum to include newly observed property types and add monitoring for additional edge cases.
options.explain: Whack-a-mole enum maintenance — every new listing style risks another validation failure.

options.text: Add an "other" value to your enum with a separate `property_type_detail` string field for specifics when "other" is selected.
options.explain: Correct. Keeps the strong enum for the common cases (clean downstream joins) while giving a well-typed escape hatch that preserves detail. Stable long-term.

options.text: Change `property_type` from an enum to a free-form string and implement a normalization step in post-processing.
options.explain: Loses the validation guarantee entirely and pushes the normalization problem downstream with no canonical vocabulary.

options.text: Add few-shot examples to your prompt demonstrating how to map unexpected property types to the closest existing enum value.
options.explain: Forcing 'tiny house' into 'house' drops real distinctions downstream — you're silently lossy.

text:
Your extraction system parses e-commerce product descriptions to extract specifications like dimensions, weight, and materials into JSON. Despite having a well-defined schema, the model inconsistently extracts the "materials" field—sometimes returning "cotton blend", other times "Cotton/Polyester mix", and occasionally omitting the field when material information is clearly present in the source.
What's the most effective way to improve extraction consistency?

options.text: Make the "materials" field required instead of optional in the schema to force the model to always extract a value
options.explain: Required-ness doesn't solve format inconsistency, and it can force invented values when material info is genuinely missing.

options.text: Switch to a more capable model tier since inconsistent extraction indicates insufficient model capability
options.explain: Costly, and doesn't address the actual cause — the model doesn't know the canonical output format you want.

options.text: Set temperature to 0 to eliminate randomness and ensure deterministic outputs
options.explain: Determinism doesn't define the right format; it just makes the wrong-format answers consistent.

options.text: Add few-shot examples showing 2-3 complete input-output pairs with standardized material description formats
options.explain: Correct. Few-shot examples demonstrate the exact canonical format you want ('cotton/polyester' as a normalized list), and they also raise recall on material info that was being skipped.

text:
Documents arrive continuously throughout business hours and need structured data extracted. To reduce costs, you want to use the `Message Batches API` (50% discount, up-to-24-hour processing window). Your SLA specifies that extraction results must be available within 30 hours of document arrival with 99.9% reliability.
Which batching strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Submit batches every 6 hours containing documents from that window
options.explain: Worst-case a document waits 6 hours to be batched and then up to 24 hours to process = 30 hours exactly. No safety margin for a 99.9% reliability target.

options.text: Submit a single batch at end of day containing all documents from that day
options.explain: A doc arriving just after morning startup waits all day to be batched plus up to 24 hours to process — well over 30 hours.

options.text: Submit batches every 4 hours containing documents from that window
options.explain: Correct. Max 4-hour wait + up to 24-hour batch SLO = 28-hour worst case, leaving a 2-hour cushion under the 30-hour SLA to absorb batch variance and hit 99.9%.

options.text: Use the real-time API for all documents instead of batch processing
options.explain: Meets SLA trivially but throws away the 50% cost saving the question asks you to capture.

text:
After deployment, you find that 12% of extractions contain semantic errors that pass JSON schema validation (e.g., a duration like "30 minutes" incorrectly placed in an ingredient quantity field). Human reviewers have capacity to check only 20% of extractions.
Which approach most effectively allocates reviewer attention?

options.text: Have the model output field-level confidence scores, then calibrate review thresholds using a labeled validation set.
options.explain: Correct. Field-level confidence lets you route the low-confidence 20% — which is where the 12% semantic errors concentrate — to humans. Calibration makes the threshold choice data-driven.

options.text: Randomly sample 20% of extractions for review, using corrections to track accuracy and identify error patterns.
options.explain: Great for measurement, poor for coverage — random sampling only catches 20% of the 12% errors (~2.4% total). Confidence-based routing finds far more.

options.text: Prioritize review of all extractions where required fields are empty or explicitly marked as not found.
options.explain: Empty fields are a narrow error mode. The 12% problem is wrong values in populated fields, which this filter misses.

options.text: Review all extractions from documents with formatting anomalies such as unusual layouts or mixed content types.
options.explain: A useful heuristic, but formatting anomaly doesn't reliably predict where fields got confused with each other.

text:
After your daily batch of 10,000 documents completes, 300 documents (3%) failed with "`context_length_exceeded`" errors. The results file identifies each failure by `custom_id`.
What's the most cost-effective approach to process these failures?

options.text: Reprocess the entire batch with prompt caching enabled to reduce the cost of retrying requests with identical system prompts
options.explain: Re-running 10,000 successful documents to solve 300 failures is wasteful regardless of caching savings. And caching doesn't fix the length-exceeded error.

options.text: Resubmit only the 300 failed documents after chunking them into smaller pieces, then combine the partial extractions
options.explain: Correct. Targeted, and it addresses the actual cause (input too long). Chunk the oversized docs, extract per chunk, then merge — minimum tokens, fixes the specific failure mode.

options.text: Resubmit the entire 10,000 document batch using a model tier with a larger context window
options.explain: Expensive (reprocessing 9,700 successes) and may still fail on truly outsized documents.

options.text: Increase the `max_tokens` parameter for the 300 failed documents and resubmit them in a new batch
options.explain: `max_tokens` controls output length, not input length. It doesn't solve `context_length_exceeded`, which is about the combined input-plus-output budget.
